topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My stomach clenched, a tight knot forming beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, my fingers trembling slightly as I scanned the subject line: "Regarding your recent piece, [My Name]." The content was a cold, hard declaration: my article, the one I’d poured weeks of late nights and meticulous research into, had been published under the name of a complete stranger. A slow, hot wave of disbelief washed over me, quickly followed by a sharp, stinging resentment. I reread the text, my eyes blurring slightly. Who had done this? I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my apartment, the floorboards groaning under my hurried steps. Every muscle in my jaw felt tight, clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my temples. I wanted to scream, to lash out, but the words caught in my throat, leaving only a dry, rasping sound escaping my lips.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my breath catching in shallow gasps. The realization hit me with the force of a physical shove. My carefully crafted narrative, the voice I had so painstakingly honed, belonged to someone else. A deep, churning nausea rose in my throat, making me instinctively reach for the cool surface of the desk. My hands shook uncontrollably as I tried to process the audacity of it all. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic protesting beneath my weight, and let out a long, slow exhale that sounded more like a defeated sigh. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over the contact for the editor, a muscle twitching near my eye. The silence in the room was heavy, oppressive, amplifying the dull throb behind my eyes. I wanted to smash something, anything, just to release the pressure building up inside me.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, I physically recoiled, knocking over my lukewarm coffee. The liquid spread across the keyboard, mirroring the mess inside my head. My jaw locked so tightly I could feel the tendons strain in my neck. I didn't move for a full minute, just staring at the screen, the text mocking me with its unauthorized presence. A low, guttural sound escaped my chest—a sound devoid of any real warmth. I slammed my fist onto the desk, the impact jarring my wrist. The sound echoed in the quiet office, loud and jarring. My gaze darted around the room, searching for an escape route, a place to go where this sickening sense of violation wouldn't cling to me. The air felt thick, suffocating, and I felt utterly powerless.
